title: Embedding living larvae in low-melting agarose for imaging and recovery thereafter
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"75lk3","text":"This protocol describes how to use agarose with a low gelling point (27-30°C; often called low-melting agarose) to immobilize Aiptasia larvae for microscopy. Larvae stay alive and in one place for prolonged periods of several hours but continue to mainly spin around the longitudal axis. After imaging, larvae can be retrieved from the agarose and transferred back to artificial sea water.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9tj2r","text":"This is an updated version of this protocol with some minor changes and includes now the instructions how to make VALAP and how to use activated charcoal to limit the search area under the cover slip.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"aflm","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7j32f","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"iph5","text":"A protocol for making the Valap for sealing the sample can be found under the following link: http://cshprotocols.cshlp.org/content/2010/12/pdb.rec12380","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":94,"length":58}],"data":[]},{"key":"2lf37","text":"Valap is basically a wax-like substance which has the advantages over other ways of sealing (for example nail-polish) that it is hydrophobic and doesn't contain any solvents which might mix with the aqueous sample and interfere with viability and fluorescence.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"82u4t","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5a2a6","text":"We are actually using a device with a small resistance wire loop to take a small amount of cold, solid Valap and then to melt it directly at the slide-mounted cover slip.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":170}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"DDCA3CAECD404C6D913131BCCD4CF5AB","url":"http://cshprotocols.cshlp.org/content/2010/12/pdb.rec12380"}}}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"2sfku","text":"Melt one (and an optional second one for adding activated charcoal) aliquot of 2% low-melting point agarose at 65°C. Once melted, vortex briefly and place at 37°C (ideally in a heat block directly next to the working area with the stereo-microscope).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9t72e","text":"OPTIONAL: Add a small amount of fine activated charcoal to the second aliquot and vortex thoroughly until the aliquot appears uniformly black, place back at 37°C.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"epnq1","text":"Be careful when melting the agarose in the microwave. Never use a closed container. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

Aliquot into small glass jars and let solidify.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"b9j8g","text":"Valap is basically a wax-like substance which has the advantages over other ways of sealing (for example nail-polish) that it is hydrophobic and doesn't contain any solvents which might mix with the aqueous sample and interfere with viability and fluorescence.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":260}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"enitd","text":"The protocol for making the Valap for sealing the sample can be found under the following link: http://cshprotocols.cshlp.org/content/2010/12/pdb.rec12380","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":96}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":96,"length":58}],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"DDCA3CAECD404C6D913131BCCD4CF5AB","url":"http://cshprotocols.cshlp.org/content/2010/12/pdb.rec12380"}}}}
steps: Before usage, melt the 1 ml agarose aliquot at 65°C in either a water bath or a heat block. This takes only a few minutes for the small aliquot. Melting the agarose in the 50 ml tube takes a little longer. 2% agarose stock (so 1% end concentration) works best in regards to keeping the larvae in place and being easy to pipette and mix. Higher concentrations (up to 8% for the stock) have no obvious benefit but make pipetting and mixing difficult.
Place the melted agarose at 37°C to keep it liquid and ready for use.
Label the microscope slide, prepare a coverslip with clay feet and place both next to a stereomicroscope.

Under the stereomicroscope transfer your larvae in 10 to 20 µl ASW into a siliconized 1.5-mL microcentrifuge tube and close the lid. 20 µl should allow to take up 20-30 larvae with practice but for more concentrate them by a series of pipetting steps into smaller and smaller volumes of ASW, that way 50-100 larvae can be embedded under one cover slip. Larger volumes fit under standard 18×18 mm coverslips but at the expense of having the larvae in different planes and imaging through a lot of agarose for some of them.
Adjust a second micropipette to double the volume you have your larvae in.
Place the microscope slide under the stereomicroscope and adjust focus and magnification to be able to later see the larvae on top.

Take the 37°C warm agarose and place it next to the stereomicroscope and your larvae. The agarose will cool down to room temperature quickly, so you can directly proceed to the next step. However, if you are worried about heat stress you can use a 27°C heat block and wait a few minutes. ATTENTION: If you wait too long, the agarose will solidify at this temperature.
Add one volume of agarose to your larvae and immediately mix by gently pipetting up and down several times. Place the agarose back to 37°C. E.g. , to 20 µL ASW with larvae, add 20 µL agarose.

Take the second pipette and transfer the complete mix to the microscope slide and, while watching through the microscope, put the cover slip on top and press it a little bit down to obtain a roughly larval-thick layer of agarose. CAUTION: Be careful to keep all larvae under the cover slip and not to squeeze them too much. Make also sure to apply the cover slip with the clay feet below, otherwise it will squash the larvae immediately!

After 3-5 min, when the agarose on the slide has solidified, add some of the 2% agarose to the edges of the coverslip to fill the complete area under it by capillary action. It helps to dedicate a separate aliquot of 2% agarose for this and mix in a small amount (tip of a small spatula) of fine activated charcoal. This prevents from searching larvae in areas where there can't be any but doesn't interfere with any fluorescence imaging.
Seal the edges of the coverslip airtight with hot VALAP.
After sealing check the larvae a last time under the stereo microscope.

Take your images. The larvae should be fine for at least 2-4 h. To not get confused which larvae were already imaged, do a manual raster across the area with larvae (e.g., start in upper right one corner, search for larvae by going through the whole column down, then go left so the next column will be with a slight overlap to the previous one and go up, and so on).
Use another coverslip, razor blade or scalpel to remove the wax around the sample under the stereo microscope.

Use a scalpel blade to carefully and slowly lift the cover slip. Doing this under a stereo microscope helps to follow the positions of the larvae. CAUTION: As soon as you remove the cover slip the sample will dry out very fast.
Place the cover slip upside down on a clean surface.
Cover the cover slip as well as the sample area on the slide with ASW.

Locate and collect the larvae with a micropipette and transfer them back into their dish. If some larvae are still embedded in the agarose, you can just wait until they free themselves or transfer the larvae together with the small piece of agarose.
creator: Christian CR Renicke
